feed.title: New bitcoin backbone code release: BIP152, outbound tx support + libbitcoinkernel API feedbacks

entry.title: New bitcoin backbone code release: BIP152, outbound tx support + libbitcoinkernel API feedbacks
entry.summary:
The recent update shared with developers highlights significant advancements in the bitcoin backbone, now accessible on [bitcoinbackbone.org](http://bitcoinbackbone.org). The update primarily focuses on enhancing support for compact-block BIP152, alongside improvements in outbound transaction synchronization and internal mechanisms due to its native multi-process architecture. Notably, these updates also touch upon address management among other minor tweaks.
A key aspect of the current bitcoin backbone iteration is its utilization of a custom C interface designed to interact with the libbitcoinkernel's encapsulated consensus code more efficiently. This approach has been favored for its flexibility, allowing for easier updates to consensus code while benefiting from the advantages offered by a homemade interface solution.
Discussion within the developer community also touches upon the potential for further improvements in how the system interacts with blockchain data. Suggestions include the development of two interface extensions. The first proposed extension aims at enabling independent verification of header consensus structures, which could enhance the reliability of block relay and header sanitation processes. The second suggestion focuses on providing a comprehensive validation mechanism for transactions across all consensus checks during mempool processing. Such capabilities would potentially allow for a more nuanced handling of unconfirmed transaction chains, tracing them back to their origin coinbase or deeply buried UTXO, thus supporting more sophisticated mempool management strategies.
These discussions reflect ongoing efforts to refine and expand the bitcoin backbone's functionality, with a particular focus on debugging capabilities and the management of chain state information. By exploring these interface extensions, developers aim to improve access to lower-level data and enhance the system's overall utility for debugging purposes and internal state checks.
